original_abstract: There are few behavioral effects as ubiquitous as the speed-accuracy tradeoff (SAT). From insects to rodents to primates, the tendency for decision speed to covary with decision accuracy seems an inescapable property of choice behavior. Recently, the SAT has received renewed interest, as neuroscience approaches begin to uncover its neural underpinnings and computational models are compelled to incorporate it as a necessary benchmark. The present work provides a comprehensive overview of SAT. First, I trace its history as a tractable behavioral phenomenon and the role it has played in shaping mathematical descriptions of the decision process. Second, I present a "users guide" of SAT methodology, including a critical review of common experimental manipulations and analysis techniques and a treatment of the typical behavioral patterns that emerge when SAT is manipulated directly. Finally, I review applications of this methodology in several domains.
humanized_abstract: One of the most ubiquitous effects of behavior is the speed-accuracy tradeoff (SAT). From insects to rodents to primates, faster decisions are almost invariably accompanied by worse accuracy and slower decisions by better accuracy. Recent advances in neuroscience methods are starting to uncover the neuronal components of the SAT, while computational models are being forced to confront this ubiquitous behavioral effect. Here I summarize the effects, history and influence of the SAT, as well as providing a "users guide" for experimental and analytical methods for studying it. I also review the behavior that results from directly manipulating components of the SAT, and finally I review some of the many applications of this method.

Here I summarize the effects, history and influence of the SAT, as well as providing a "users guide" for experimental and analytical methods for studying it. I also review the behavior that results from directly manipulating components of the SAT, and finally I review some of the many applications of this method.
